EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal boundary interacted with a seasonably moist airmass resulting in multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms that resulted in one to three inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patterson Creek Village Pike closed due to high water near Plum Run Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pressure trough ignited a few thunderstorms that produced spotty wind damage during the late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees blew down on CR-12 Howards Lick Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees blew down on CR-16 Mill Gap Road east of Lost River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pressure trough ignited a few thunderstorms that produced spotty wind damage during the late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage was reported in Delray, including a tree that blew down on WV-29 Delray Road near CR-7/1 North Texas Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system gained strength west of the Ohio River on January 12th as a moist system approaching from the south congealed with a disturbance sinking down from the Upper Midwest. Much colder air filtered in with the system as it traveled into West Virginia, transitioning onset rain over to snow across much of the state. Accumulating snow began along the higher terrain before dawn on the morning of the 13th while the lower elevations did not see the transition over to snow until a few hours later. Overall snow totals ranged from 1 to 3 inches around the Charleston metro and along the I-79 corridor and 3 to 5 inches along the mountains. Snow showers tapered down on the 14th once high pressure built into the area.

Snow/sleet accumulations totaled a few tenths to two inches and ice accumulations totaled a few hundredths to one tenth of an inch. This led to several accidents.

A snow squall with visibilities less than one quarter of a mile and winds gusting in excess of 30 mph quickly accumulated up to one inch of snow in western and central Mineral County, WV.

Surface low pressure progressed through West Virginia on the evening of January 8th and continued to promote showers through the morning of the 9th. Warmer temperatures at the onset of showers led to rain across the southern portions of the state, while a wintry mix took shape along the higher terrain on the evening of the 8th. Temperatures fell back below freezing that night, transforming lingering precipitation into snow. This system then quickly departed towards New England after daybreak on the 9th.||During the course of this event, a light glaze of ice was observed by local cooperative observers due to the freezing rain. This led to slick roadways and ice along tree branches and vehicles.

In the wake of a cold front, a strong low pressure system ushered in colder air into the Central Appalachians on the evening of January 30th into the early morning hours of the 31st. This prompted a transition from rain to a wintry mix of precipitation across West Virginia during the overnight hours. The influx of winter weather, coupled with cooling road temperatures, led to minor disruptions of the morning work commute and school delays and closures on the 31st. Icy conditions on the road led to early morning car accidents along Route 35 in Putnam County and reports of a semi-truck ran into a barrier along the Washington Street overpass on Interstate 64 in Charleston. As temperatures rose for the day, lingering precipitation transitioned back over to all rain.||Freezing rain resulted in a tenth of an inch of ice accumulations in portions of the state, with the majority of airports measuring freezing rain at the airfield. The Yeager Airport had 0.10 inches of freezing rain and the Huntington Airport measured 0.17 inches. The ASOS at the Parkersburg airport measured just under 0.05 inches of freezing rain before switching over to a mix of light sleet and slow right after midnight.

Ahead of a cold front passing through the state on the evening of January 19th, gusty winds persisted across West Virginia. A tightened pressure gradient yielded strong winds during the afternoon and evening hours of the 19th before the frontal passage.||The Automated Surface Observing System at the Yeager Airport in Charleston recorded a 48 mile per hour gust just after sunset as the winds continued to howl. Main impacts observed during this time were a few trees blown down onto roadways within the town of Danville in Boone County. Other stations in the state also measured strong winds on the upwards of 45-48 miles per hour throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down in Danville, blocking one lane of Lick Creek Road and both lanes of Daniel Boone Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope snow showers persisted along the West Virginia mountains from January 22nd to the 23rd as an upper level disturbance pivoted through the area. This led to an estimation of 4 inches of snow at the Snowshoe Resort in Pocahontas County.

Rain and embedded thunderstorms arrived into West Virginia on the morning of January 12th ahead of a cold front. A few strong thunderstorms transpired in Wood County, leading to two instances of nickel and quarter sized hail just after sunrise.

A large upper level disturbance lumbered through West Virginia between January 3rd and 4th, leading to persistent rain showers across the Ohio River Valley. As rain continued to fall in northwest West Virginia, coupled with excessive rainfall from upstream, Middle Island Creek in Tyler County rose out of its banks. This led to minor flooding of adjacent fields and roadways near the creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Middle Island Creek in the town of Little rose above its 14 foot bankfull level on the evening of January 3rd. The creek crested at 14.29 feet an hour later and then returned to its banks twenty-four hours later. While out of its banks, Stewarts Run Road observed minor flooding as did the field adjacent to the creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system gained strength west of the Ohio River on January 12th as a moist system approaching from the south congealed with a disturbance sinking down from the Upper Midwest. Much colder air filtered in with the system as it traveled into West Virginia, transitioning onset rain over to snow across much of the state. Accumulating snow began along the higher terrain before dawn on the morning of the 13th while the lower elevations did not see the transition over to snow until a few hours later. Overall snow totals ranged from 1 to 3 inches around the Charleston metro and along the I-79 corridor and 3 to 5 inches along the mountains. Snow showers tapered down on the 14th once high pressure built into the area.

In the wake of a cold front, a strong low pressure system ushered in colder air into the Central Appalachians on the evening of January 30th into the early morning hours of the 31st. This prompted a transition from rain to a wintry mix of precipitation across West Virginia during the overnight hours. The influx of winter weather, coupled with cooling road temperatures, led to minor disruptions of the morning work commute and school delays and closures on the 31st. Icy conditions on the road led to early morning car accidents along Route 35 in Putnam County and reports of a semi-truck ran into a barrier along the Washington Street overpass on Interstate 64 in Charleston. As temperatures rose for the day, lingering precipitation transitioned back over to all rain.||Freezing rain resulted in a tenth of an inch of ice accumulations in portions of the state, with the majority of airports measuring freezing rain at the airfield. The Yeager Airport had 0.10 inches of freezing rain and the Huntington Airport measured 0.17 inches. The ASOS at the Parkersburg airport measured just under 0.05 inches of freezing rain before switching over to a mix of light sleet and slow right after midnight.

A cold front slowed as it approached the Appalachian Mountains, intersecting a hot, humid, and unstable airmass. This resulted in scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms, several of which became severe producing widely scattered instances of wind damage and isolated large hail. A couple of localized considerable instances of wind damage were noted near Washington, DC, and its western suburbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines blew down in Ranson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washington Street near Charles Town Casino was closed due to multiple trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked up the Eastern Seaboard from the Carolinas on January 22nd to the New England coast on the 23rd.  Southwest flow and jet ascent created widespread precipitation across the Upper Ohio Valley from the afternoon of the 22nd to the morning of the 23rd.  Most of the snow accumulation was focused in areas north of I-70 in eastern Ohio and southwest Pennsylvania, save for some accumulations in the usual ridge locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Terra Alta received a total of 5.3 inches of snow.

Most of the snow accumulation was focused in areas north of I-70 in eastern Ohio and southwest Pennsylvania, save for some accumulations in the usual ridge locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Davis received 4.2 inches of snow in total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front remained relatively stationary across the Upper Ohio Valley on January 3rd. An anomalously moist and warm air mass surged northward into the zone of convergence associated with the boundary, and combined with elevated instability to create enhanced rainfall rates.  Marshall County received a total of 1.25 to 3 inches of rain from this event, but only one incident of stream flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fish Creek was reported to be out of its banks, and it was flooding Fish Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ripples of vorticity in a large-scale trough kept numerous snow showers falling across the Upper Ohio Valley on January 26th into the early morning hours of the 27th.  Steep low-level lapse rates and strong cold advection helped to briefly enhance snow rates at times. Most accumulations were in the 1 to 5 inch range, highest in the West Virginia ridges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 4.7 inches was reported at Terra Alta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold northwest wind behind an arctic cold front caused wind chills to continue to range between -10 and -20 degrees during the afternoon of the 3rd into the morning of the 4th.

An area of low pressure moved through North Carolina before tracking northeast off the coast of Virginia. North of the area of low pressure, a mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing rain developed. Ice accumulations totaled a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch and snow accumulations totaled a coating to four inches. The greatest impacts were above 2,000 ft AGL, where numerous trees were downed due to the ice. Several accidents and isolated power outages were reported.

A wave of wintry precipitation developed to the north of a frontal boundary with temperatures near or just below freezing. This resulted in a period of freezing rain, sleet, and snow. Ice accumulations totaled a coating to a few hundredths of an inch and snow accumulations totaled a coating to one inch.

A stationary front located near West Virginia on the morning of February 16th acted as a link between two low pressure systems streaming north and south of the area. Early morning thunderstorms promoted locally heavy rain, with a second round taking place later that evening as the front made eastward progress. ||Prolonged periods of rain led to flooding across the central and southern portions of the state. Most notable impacts from flooding occurred in Milton, where over one hundred homes observed basement flooding and high water surrounding the neighborhood. One to three inches of rain fell during this time, maintaining saturated conditions and flooding concerns on February 17th and into the next day before the floodwaters began to recede.||In Fayette County, an infant passed away due to a car being swept away from fast moving water near Paint Creek.||Several rivers rose above their banks in the midst of the event, including parts of the Mud River, Coal River, and Guyandotte River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was first observed early in the afternoon of February 16th as Sugartree Fork rose above its banks, resulting in high water along the bridge at the intersection of Sycamore Road and Sugartree Road. Nearby in the towns of Yawkey and Griffithsville, high water obstructed traffic along portions of Route 124 and Route 3 as Porter Fork and Straight Fork rose out of their banks. In Hamlin, the Mud River overflowed onto Route 7 and intersecting smaller roads, which closed the road for a period of time.||The next morning, ongoing rain resulted in additional flooding in Hamlin and West Hamlin. Trace Fork rose out of its banks during this time near the Lincoln/Putnam County line and flooded sections of Route 34. Additionally, One Mile Creek rose out of its banks and spilled onto Dr. Steele Farm Road near West Hamlin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed in spots along Route 119 in the southern portions of Roane County. This included at the intersection with Cottontree Road in the town of Cotton as well as at the intersection of Charleston Road in Walton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Hamlin, the gage along the Mud River observed a rise above the river's 13 foot bankfull level on the afternoon of February 16th. The river crested at 15.29 feet just before sunrise on the 17th and did not return to its banks until the early morning hours of February 18th. During this prolonged period of being out of its banks, the river caused flooding along sections of Upper Mud River Road as well as some homes along and near the Mud River. Sections of Route 7 between Myra and Hamlin, and sections of Route 3 on either side of Hamlin, also flooded. The ball fields near Lincoln County High School were under water during this time as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tug Fork in Williamson rose above its 27 foot flood stage late in the afternoon on February 17th. The river crested at 28.7 feet later that evening and returned to its banks a few hours after midnight on the 18th. While above its flood stage, backwater from the Tug Fork spilled onto Route 612 in South Williamson and caused minor traffic obstructions. The water treatment plant in Williamson closed its flood gate to help prevent water from the river to spill into the area until the floodwaters receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged rainfall over a 24- to 25-hour period ended around 8 AM EST on February 17th, which resulted in localized minor river flooding across portions of southeast West Virginia (SEWV).  Unusual warmth during most of the river meant no frozen ground, snow cover or river ice in the area.  However, MRMS CREST indicated that the ground was still 50 to 75 percent saturated from widespread rainfall during the 12th and 13th, just a few days prior.  On the morning of the 16th, SEWV was located on the south side of a stationary front draped near the Ohio River.  Strong southwesterly windflow carried deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico across the area, with precipitable water values (PWATs) in excess of 1.0 inch through much of the day on the 16th.  PWATs would increase to around 1.2 inches by sunrise of the 17th, just as a supporting upper level trough began to push across the Appalachian chain.  Rainfall rates were generally under a quarter inch per hour on the 16th, but began to increase during the overnight hours as the trough approached, in some instances exceeding an inch per hour for brief periods of time as a few thunderstorms developed, supported by Most-Unstable Layer CAPE values increasing to over 1,000 J/Kg ahead of the approaching trough.  Total rainfall amounts through the event ranged from 2.25 to locally 3.50 inches, resulting in minor river flooding along the Greenbrier, Bluestone and Meadow Rivers, as well as Anthony Creek.  Other smaller tributaries experienced minor flooding as well.  No flash flooding occurred.  Reported impacts were limited to flooded roads with little to no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station (Weather Underground:KWVRAINE18) recorded 2.52 inches of rainfall during at 25 hour period ending 8:00 AM EST on 2/17/2023. This was across ground already highly moistened from periods of heavy rain during the 12th & 13th a few days before. Rainfall rates at times were observed up to 0.5 inch per hour. This amount was between a 5-year and a 10-year event (20%-10% AEP) per NOAA Atlas 14 point precipitation frequency estimates. Runoff from this location flows into Sewell Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a stacked weather disturbance encroached on the region, winds went from light to quite gusty beginning on the morning of February 9th. The highest winds were observed later that afternoon, which resulted in isolated tree damage in Kanawha and Upshur Counties. Gusty conditions continued overnight before calming down the next morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and a few power lines were blown down in the city of Charleston and the town of Cross Lanes.

Prolonged rainfall over a 24- to 25-hour period ended around 8 AM EST on February 17th, which resulted in localized minor river flooding across portions of southeast West Virginia (SEWV).  Unusual warmth during most of the river meant no frozen ground, snow cover or river ice in the area.  However, MRMS CREST indicated that the ground was still 50 to 75 percent saturated from widespread rainfall during the 12th and 13th, just a few days prior.  On the morning of the 16th, SEWV was located on the south side of a stationary front draped near the Ohio River.  Strong southwesterly windflow carried deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico across the area, with precipitable water values (PWATs) in excess of 1.0 inch through much of the day on the 16th.  PWATs would increase to around 1.2 inches by sunrise of the 17th, just as a supporting upper level trough began to push across the Appalachian chain.  Rainfall rates were generally under a quarter inch per hour on the 16th, but began to increase during the overnight hours as the trough approached, in some instances exceeding an inch per hour for brief periods of time as a few thunderstorms developed, supported by Most-Unstable Layer CAPE values increasing to over 1,000 J/Kg ahead of the approaching trough.  Total rainfall amounts through the event ranged from 2.25 to locally 3.50 inches, resulting in minor river flooding along the Greenbrier, Bluestone and Meadow Rivers, as well as Anthony Creek.  Other smaller tributaries experienced minor flooding as well.  No flash flooding occurred.  Reported impacts were limited to flooded roads with little to no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station (Weather Underground:KWVBLUEF24) recorded 2.39 inches of rainfall during a little more than 24 hours ending 8:00 AM EST on 2/17/2023. This was across ground already highly moistened from periods of heavy rain during the 12th & 13th a few days before. Rainfall rates at times were observed up to 0.5 inch per hour. This amount was about a 2-year event (50%-10% AEP) per NOAA Atlas 14 point precipitation frequency estimates. Runoff from this location flows into the Bluestone River.

Ice accumulations totaled a few hundredths to two tenths of an inch and snow accumulations totaled a coating to four inches. The greatest impacts were above 2,000 ft AGL, where numerous trees were downed due to the ice. Several accidents and isolated power outages were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gage along the Coal River in Tornado observed a rise above the river's 25 foot flood stage on the afternoon of February 17th. The river crested at 25.31 feet that evening and returned to its banks just after midnight on the 18th. While in flood, the river caused portions of Smith Creek Road to close due to the high water concerns. Other sections of Coal River Road upstream from the Tornado bridge were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged rainfall over a 24- to 25-hour period ended around 8 AM EST on February 17th, which resulted in localized minor river flooding across portions of southeast West Virginia (SEWV).  Unusual warmth during most of the river meant no frozen ground, snow cover or river ice in the area.  However, MRMS CREST indicated that the ground was still 50 to 75 percent saturated from widespread rainfall during the 12th and 13th, just a few days prior.  On the morning of the 16th, SEWV was located on the south side of a stationary front draped near the Ohio River.  Strong southwesterly windflow carried deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico across the area, with precipitable water values (PWATs) in excess of 1.0 inch through much of the day on the 16th.  PWATs would increase to around 1.2 inches by sunrise of the 17th, just as a supporting upper level trough began to push across the Appalachian chain.  Rainfall rates were generally under a quarter inch per hour on the 16th, but began to increase during the overnight hours as the trough approached, in some instances exceeding an inch per hour for brief periods of time as a few thunderstorms developed, supported by Most-Unstable Layer CAPE values increasing to over 1,000 J/Kg ahead of the approaching trough.  Total rainfall amounts through the event ranged from 2.25 to locally 3.50 inches, resulting in minor river flooding along the Greenbrier, Bluestone and Meadow Rivers, as well as Anthony Creek.  Other smaller tributaries experienced minor flooding as well.  No flash flooding occurred.  Reported impacts were limited to flooded roads with little to no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service employee reported signs that Anthony Creek, as well as multiple tributaries of the creek were out of their banks and covering Highway 92 near the community of Neola, WV during the 17th.  No damage was reported, though removal of debris was likely necessary.

No snow melt or frozen ground was involved, but the ground was moist from widespread rainfall on the 12th and 13th a few days prior. Water was observed creeping up to Highway 19 in the Spanishburg area, but there were no reports that the highway was actually closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front located near West Virginia on the morning of February 16th acted as a link between two low pressure systems streaming north and south of the area. Early morning thunderstorms promoted locally heavy rain, with a second round taking place later that evening as the front made eastward progress. ||Prolonged periods of rain led to flooding across the central and southern portions of the state. Most notable impacts from flooding occurred in Milton, where over one hundred homes observed basement flooding and high water surrounding the neighborhood. One to three inches of rain fell during this time, maintaining saturated conditions and flooding concerns on February 17th and into the next day before the floodwaters began to recede.||In Fayette County, an infant passed away due to a car being swept away from fast moving water near Paint Creek.||Several rivers rose above their banks in the midst of the event, including parts of the Mud River, Coal River, and Guyandotte River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continuous rounds of rain led to prolonged flooding around the Pax area. High water was first observed on the evening of February 16th along Packs Branch Road and portions of Paint Creek Road. The next morning, Paint Creek remained out of its banks and continued to spill onto Paint Creek Road. A vehicle drove into the flooded road and was swept away along with an infant. Local authorities later found the infant had drowned.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds, ranging between 45 and 55mph, were measured in the ridges of eastern Tucker County between 2pm and 5pm on the afternoon of February 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe line of thunderstorms, followed by high sustained wind gusts in the ridges, passed through the region on February 27th. ||Instability was limited by the time a cold front advanced through the region due to the time of the day, limited low level moisture, and earlier shower activity with the warm front. However low-level wind shear pushed convective storms to move at 60mph near I-70.  Wind gusts ranged between 40 to 55mph in portions of the Ohio River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 52mph wind gust was measured at Morgantown Airport at 803pm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts, between 45 and 55mph, were measured in eastern Tucker above 4,000ft in the early morning hours of February 24th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill as low as -26F was measured in the ridges of eastern Tucker County on the night of February 3rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage at Anthony Creek near Anthony (ANYW2) crested at 15.87 feet (7,865 cfs) at 1:20 pm EST on 2/17/23.  This is roughly a 2-year event (50% AEP) per USGS Streamstats for this location and the highest crest on record since 2014.  The Anthony Creek basin received between 1.75 and 2.5 inches of rainfall in about a 24-hour period ending 8:00 AM EST on 2/17/23, falling over ground that was moistened by widespread rainfall on the 12th and 13th a few days prior.  There was no snow cover prior to this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With clear conditions during the early morning hours of February 22nd prompting radiational cooling, dry air at the surface (dew points in the 20s), and a passing warm front, freezing rain was observed for a few hours at isolated areas as surface temperatures were near or just below freezing. Warm air advection and diurnal heating created icy conditions to cease by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain was observed the morning of February 22nd in portions of Ohio County. About 0.02 inches of radial ice was measured at Wheeling Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface low pressure tracked across Michigan and into eastern Canada on the 25th, along a track that is climatologically favored for strong wind events in the Upper Ohio Valley.  A crossing occluded boundary was a catalyst to start mixing strong wind to the surface, and a tight pressure gradient kept gusty wind going into the early evening. Several instances of strong wind gusts and wind damage were noted in northern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts of up to 54 MPH were reported at a mesonet sensor near Canaan Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A crossing shortwave trough and deepening surface low spread precipitation across the area on March 10th. Precipitation started mainly as rain, but then transitioned to snow during the night of the 10th and the morning of the 11th as the low crossed and cold advection set in.  Some accumulation was noted in the higher elevations of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 1 to 1.5 inches was reported in the Terra Alta area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across the region on the 13th. Antecedent moisture and synoptic lift |created some convective snow showers with brief low visibility during the daytime hours, but a warm ground kept accumulations in check initially. Overnight, continued shortwave enhancement maintained snow over much of the region, and the cooling temperatures and ground surface allowed for additional accumulations.  A secondary shortwave trough continued some mainly light snow on the 14th before snow ended during the evening. Upslope enhancement and colder surface temperatures helped to add to snow totals on the ridges as opposed to the lowlands, with system total snow ending up being one of the higher totals of the snow season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer near Terra Alta reported a total of 7.1 inches for the event. Four inches was the total 7 miles north of Thomas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the Lower Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 3rd towards the northeast U.S. coast on the morning of the 4th. Although the best potential for damaging wind gusts was in southwest Pennsylvania, some strong wind gusts and damage were noted in northern West Virginia, particularly Preston and Tucker Counties, due to a strong pressure gradient.  Wind gusts of up to 69 MPH were measured on the peaks of eastern Tucker during the early morning hours of the 4th, and areas of widespread tree and power line damage were received from both counties. More isolated incidents were noted in other West Virginia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Route 218 South near Helens Run, blocking traffic in both directions.  Near White Hall, a road sign was blown down on to a vehicle, with no injuries reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of up to 49 MPH were measured at Wheeling Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the Lower Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 3rd towards the northeast U.S. coast on the morning of the 4th. Although the best potential for damaging wind gusts was in southwest Pennsylvania, some strong wind gusts and damage were noted in northern West Virginia, particularly Preston and Tucker Counties, due to a strong pressure gradient.  Wind gusts of up to 69 MPH were measured on the peaks of eastern Tucker during the early morning hours of the 4th, and areas of widespread tree and power line damage were received from both counties. More isolated incidents were noted in other West Virginia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the area of Bruceton Mills, a large number of trees and power lines were downed, and numerous areas were without power for a time.

Wind gusts of up to 69 MPH were measured on the peaks of eastern Tucker during the early morning hours of the 4th, and areas of widespread tree and power line damage were received from both counties. More isolated incidents were noted in other West Virginia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread downed trees and power lines were reported.  Reported concentrations include along Glade Farm Road and Hazleton Road near Hazleton, and near Aurora, where a large number of pine trees were downed along with power lines. A more isolated incident of a downed tree on West Virginia Route 7 near Caddell was also reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts as high as 69 MPH were reported at a mesonet sensor 5 miles east of Canaan Heights, as well as by a trained spotter near Davis. Numerous trees and power lines were reported down in both Red Creek and Jenningston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep mixing between a departing cold front and a secondary frontal passage supported gusty to strong winds across West Virginia to open up the month of April. Winds picked up in intensity during the late morning hours and peaked in the afternoon/early evening. 40-50 miles per hour gusts were common throughout the afternoon across West Virginia, with the strongest gust recorded occurring at a mesonet station in Snowshoe of 65 mph. All major airports in the region observed a peak wind gust above 50 miles per hour that day.||Hundreds of thousands of households in the state lost power by the afternoon time frame as a result of trees blowing down onto power lines. Around the same time the Charleston Yeager airport measured a peak wind gust of 59 miles per hour, the South Charleston City Hall suffered considerable structural damage as the roof was blown off a portion of the building. In Barbour County, parts of roofs were also blown off of homes. ||Winds finally began to subside after dark on the 1st as the disturbance departed into New England and high pressure built in from the west. However, the power restoration process extended into the next several days as utility companies began work on installing new power poles and lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 10,000 homes in the county were without power as a result of blown down trees and power lines throughout Welch, Iaeger, and other townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying line of thunderstorms traveling through the Ohio Valley on April 1st arrived into West Virginia just before sunrise. While the storms remained below severe thresholds in the state, there was one instance of flooding in Lincoln County as a result of heavy downpours. High water issues arose on roadways in Danville and West Hamlin due to blocked culverts in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blocked culverts along Straight Fork Road near West Hamlin spilled onto the road and obstructed traffic. Near the town of Danville, high water was observed at the intersection of Left Fork and Bark Camp Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms transpired on April 4th and 5th as the result of strong southerly flow ushering in warmer than average temperatures into West Virginia. Storms on the 4th had the capability of producing one inch hail in Lincoln County, while on the 5th damaging wind gusts were the main culprit behind downed trees in Harrison and Taylor Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous upper-level low remained nearly stationary over the Great Lakes resulting in a period of high elevation, late-season, impactful snow for another night. Overall accumulations of 4-8 were focused on elevations above 2,500 feet. Winds gusted to 40 mph resulting in blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous spotters reported 4-8 of snow that started to accumulate near sunset on 5/2/2023 and continued through the overnight into the morning of 5/3/2023.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of showers developed along a strong cold front in western Ohio the morning of April 1st and advanced through eastern Ohio/western Pennsylvania. Widespread damage was recorded across the region. Gusts up 75mph were also reported in some areas. Despite little lightning in the line convection, the environment was favorable for strong wind gusts with CAPE values ranging between 100 to 400J/kg and a 50 to 65kt low-level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down on Johnsonville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of showers developed along a strong cold front in western Ohio the morning of April 1st and advanced through eastern Ohio/western Pennsylvania. Widespread damage was recorded across the region. Gusts up 75mph were also reported in some areas. Despite little lightning in the line convection, the environment was favorable for strong wind gusts with CAPE values ranging between 100 to 400J/kg and a 50 to 65kt low-level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on Route 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system passed north of the area on March 3rd in accompaniment with a surface cold front. Strong winds above the surface were able to mix down throughout the day outside of showers and thunderstorms, especially by the afternoon and evening timeframe. The Charleston airport measured a 60 mph wind gust around the peak of the afternoon and several other local airports reported strong to high winds. Winds began to diminish from west to east late on March 3rd, but prolonged gusts of 58 mph were observed along the West Virginia mountains into the morning hours of the 4th.||Downed trees and power lines were observed across West Virginia due to these strong winds. While most fell onto the nearby roadways, one tree in Wayne County fell onto a house in the town of Belpre. There was also an instance where a brush fire occurred in southeast Raleigh County as a result of downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Ripley, widespread power outages occurred due to downed trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure passed by to the west with another area of low pressure developing near the Chesapeake Bay. This resulted in southeasterly winds to overrun colder air near the surface and a prolonged wintry mix/freezing rain event across the Allegheny Mountains where temperatures were just below freezing. Ice accumulations totaled two to four tenths of an inch. Sleet and snow accumulations totaled upwards of 2 as well. Winds also gusted to 50 mph during the event. This led to numerous accidents, downed trees and power lines which resulted in isolated power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Greatest accretion on eastern facing slopes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just before dawn on March 24th, a line of strong to severe thunderstorms crossed through the Ohio River Valley into central West Virginia along a cold front. Strong winds nestled overhead in response to a low level jet aided in early morning storms that produced wind gusts on the upwards of 60-80 miles per hour. In Wayne County, instances of structural and tree damage were observed to several homes in the Shoals community. Over in Lincoln County, a portion of the local school observed significant roof damage from the thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Considerable tree damage along with structural damage was observed to several homes in the Shoals community. Damage was consistently blown in an easterly direction with no sign of organized rotation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds infiltrated the region on the afternoon of March 25th in the wake of a cold front. A tightened pressure gradient took residence over West Virginia during the early afternoon hours, which resulted in strong winds from aloft mixing down to the surface. Across West Virginia, the highest wind gusts that were observed occurred at the Clarksburg airport (64 mph) and at a mesonet station in Snowshoe (61 mph). Elsewhere, it was not uncommon to see winds between 45-55 mph through the course of the afternoon. ||These strong surface gusts, coupled with saturated grounds from previous rainfall, resulted in not only downed trees and power lines, but a few instances of roof damage. A local motor vehicle business in Bridgeport lost part of the roof awning that evening around the time the aforementioned 64 mph wind was observed in Clarksburg. In Roane County, strong winds destroyed a mobile home in the town of Colt Ridge, with multiple reports of additional roof damage within the neighborhood. In the midst of the strong winds, a man died after a tree fell on him in Cabell County as he attempted to secure items that were blowing in the wind.||Once the low pressure system quickly departed towards New England on the evening of March 25th, surface winds weakened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down across the county, including along Horse Creek Road and Robert C Byrd Freeway in the Julian township as well as a few near Sylvester.

Elsewhere, it was not uncommon to see winds between 45-55 mph through the course of the afternoon. ||These strong surface gusts, coupled with saturated grounds from previous rainfall, resulted in not only downed trees and power lines, but a few instances of roof damage. A local motor vehicle business in Bridgeport lost part of the roof awning that evening around the time the aforementioned 64 mph wind was observed in Clarksburg. In Roane County, strong winds destroyed a mobile home in the town of Colt Ridge, with multiple reports of additional roof damage within the neighborhood. In the midst of the strong winds, a man died after a tree fell on him in Cabell County as he attempted to secure items that were blowing in the wind.||Once the low pressure system quickly departed towards New England on the evening of March 25th, surface winds weakened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in the county, including onto Route 20 and Hangar Road. An unsecured greenhouse was blown away and damaged near the intersection of County Route 12/8 and Laurel Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system passed north of the area on March 3rd in accompaniment with a surface cold front. Strong winds above the surface were able to mix down throughout the day outside of showers and thunderstorms, especially by the afternoon and evening timeframe. The Charleston airport measured a 60 mph wind gust around the peak of the afternoon and several other local airports reported strong to high winds. Winds began to diminish from west to east late on March 3rd, but prolonged gusts of 58 mph were observed along the West Virginia mountains into the morning hours of the 4th.||Downed trees and power lines were observed across West Virginia due to these strong winds. While most fell onto the nearby roadways, one tree in Wayne County fell onto a house in the town of Belpre. There was also an instance where a brush fire occurred in southeast Raleigh County as a result of downed power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power outages due to tree and power line damage were observed near Middlebourne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and strong to severe thunderstorms sprouted in the afternoon and evening of June 3rd as a backdoor cold front approached and then pushed across the area. One inch hail fell in the towns of Pennsboro and Middlebourne, and damaging wind gusts resulted in multiple downed trees in the Pennsboro, French Creek, and Rock Cave areas. A tree struck by lightning in Salem fell on power lines. Later in the evening, another tree was struck in Parkersburg and split in two. One intersection in Beckley also flooded due to heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Gaskins Hollow Road in Doddridge County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a weak cold front the afternoon of June 3rd. With mid-level dry air prompting high DCAPE values (over 1000J/kg), damaging wind gusts were the main concern. However, hail the size of quarters fell in a few isolated storms due to higher terrain prompting a lower freezing level and a few storms mergers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down along Route 72 and River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of thunderstorms produced localized wind damage over the eastern West Virginia panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blew down onto power lines bringing down a power pole in the 400 block of CR-45/5 Eagle School Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system moving over the region, combined with a moist airmass, triggered a few late afternoon thunderstorms. One of these storms produced large hail in Mercer County.

The remnants of a frontal boundary from the previous day ignited additional widely scattered thunderstorms during the early evening hours. Most of the wind damage was localized over southern Maryland, where spotty large hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees blew down in the northern part of Mineral County near Fort Ashby toward the Potomac River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough spawned several severe thunderstorms over the far eastern West Virginia panhandle, extreme northern Virginia, and north-central Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage was reported near Shepherdstown, including a large tree that blew down on private property along CR-16/1 Gardners Lane.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported near Shenandoah Junction and Shepherdstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced localized wind damage over Hardy County, West Virginia, during the late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees blew down in the 1200 block of WV-7 South Fork Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms (Mesoscale Convective Complex, MCS) that developed over the Midwest moved across northern Maryland and eastern West Virginia producing localized wind damage overnight. The event was noteworthy due to its timing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees blew down in the Keyser and New Creek area, including on US-50 Northwestern Turnpike between WV-93 Laurel Dale Road and US-220 Cut Off Road, and on CR-16 Headsville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A subtle remnant disturbance from prior day's convection over the Midwest amplified a pre-existing lee pressure trough, igniting numerous thunderstorms. Given the excessive hot, humid, and unstable environment, these thunderstorms were able to organize into clusters and lines despite a lack of stronger wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail was reported in Inwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong upper-level high pressure over the central United States and moist southwest winds resulted in hot and humid conditions across the Mid-Atlantic that produced excessive heat conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS station in Kearneysville, West Virginia, MTGW2, measured peak heat indices above 105F. Several other mesonset stations in Jefferson County measured heat indices between 105 and 110.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final few days of July featured very hot and muggy weather across West Virginia. During peak heating hours, the atmosphere became conducive for afternoon showers and thunderstorms. Mainly isolated storm reports occurred on July 27th and 29th, while widespread damage took place on the 28th. ||On that particular day, hot temperatures and ample low level moisture yielded a convective line of activity across Ohio and Kentucky that eventually crossed into West Virginia. Strong winds accompanying the line resulted in a swath of downed trees and power lines starting in Wood County shortly before 3 PM and then spreading eastward through the bulk of central and southeastern West Virginia. ||At the Charleston Yeager International Airport, a 62 mile per hour wind gust was measured. Around that time, multiple areas of downed trees and power lines were observed in and around downtown Charleston. Storms eventually crossed out of the forecast area by the early evening.||While damaging wind gusts were the main issues during this stretch of unsettled weather, there was one instance of flash flooding on July 27th from activity passing over the western portions of Kanawha County. This led to high water reports in a few spots in and around Cross Lanes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down at Workman Branch Road outside of Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the midst of hot and muggy conditions ruling the roost over the Central Appalachians, diurnally driven showers and thunderstorms thrived near the end of July. On the afternoon of July 24th, a few storms that originated in southeast Ohio crossed over the river into West Virginia. One particular storm that passed over Wood County resulted in strong damaging winds that day and caused downed trees in and around the Parkersburg area. Locally heavy downpours also resulted in a small creek to rise out of its banks briefly in the town of Mineral Wells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Lubeck, a large tree was blown down onto Homewood Road, blocking both lanes of traffic. Several trees were observed down on the stretch of Homewood Road between Lubeck and Wadeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in West Virginia on the afternoons of July 13th through 15th in the midst of weak disturbances passing through the area.  Strong wind gusts accompanying storms resulted in multiple downed trees across the state. Flash flooding also blocked sections of a road in Raleigh County on the 13th while another isolated instance of flash flooding occurred on the 15th in Clay County. Storms tapered down with the loss of daytime heating after sunset each evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down on Wilsons Ridge Road near the town of Widen.

This led to high water reports in a few spots in and around Cross Lanes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was blown down outside the town of War. Both lanes of Rocket Boys Drive were blocked due to the downed tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in West Virginia on the afternoons of July 13th through 15th in the midst of weak disturbances passing through the area.  Strong wind gusts accompanying storms resulted in multiple downed trees across the state. Flash flooding also blocked sections of a road in Raleigh County on the 13th while another isolated instance of flash flooding occurred on the 15th in Clay County. Storms tapered down with the loss of daytime heating after sunset each evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Winfield, two trees were blown down along McLane Pike. One fell at Lime Kiln Road and the other at Lone Oak Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the midst of hot and muggy conditions ruling the roost over the Central Appalachians, diurnally driven showers and thunderstorms thrived near the end of July. On the afternoon of July 24th, a few storms that originated in southeast Ohio crossed over the river into West Virginia. One particular storm that passed over Wood County resulted in strong damaging winds that day and caused downed trees in and around the Parkersburg area. Locally heavy downpours also resulted in a small creek to rise out of its banks briefly in the town of Mineral Wells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the South Hills Golf Club in Parkersburg, large limbs were blown off of trees and fell onto the golf course.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a tree in the Smoot area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Preexisting hot and muggy conditions contributed to unsettled weather in West Virginia on both July 17th and 18th. Each afternoon observed strong to severe thunderstorms sprouting during peak heating hours, with damaging winds and a few instances of hails accompanying convection. Tree damage was the primary result of the passing storms, especially across portions of central and southern West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed trees in southeastern Kanawha County resulted in power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 9th, a weak disturbance, followed by a cold front, resulted in rounds of showers and thunderstorms throughout the day. Numerous showers and storms then developed in the afternoon, resulting in flash flooding in Fayette County. A few downed trees also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell and brought down power lines near Sofia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowed as it approached the Appalachian Mountains, intersecting a hot, humid, and unstable airmass. This resulted in scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms, several of which became severe producing widely scattered instances of wind damage and isolated large hail. A couple of localized considerable instances of wind damage were noted near Washington, DC, and its western suburbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees blew down along the railroad tracks south of Charles Town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front, driven by an unusually strong upper-level trough and wind shear, interacted with a hot, humid, and unstable airmass over the Mid-Atlantic. This pattern resulted in the formation of lines of thunderstorms and several supercells. The most widespread and significant wind damage was noted over northern Maryland, although pockets of considerable wind damage were noted over portions of northwestern Virginia and eastern West Virginia. Somewhat atypical for the Mid-Atlantic is late summer was the number of large hail reports, with multiple instances of giant hail noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees blew down along CR-2 Reynolds Gap Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms were observed in West Virginia on the afternoon of August 7th. Storms developed as a result of hot and humid conditions in advance of a cold front, then activity continued as the front crossed the region. Gusty winds within storms downed numerous trees and power lines, with a few instances of hail up to one and a half inch in size. Heavy rainfall led to flash flooding around Parkersburg and Lubeck in Wood County, and the towns of Lumberport and Bridgeport in Harrison County.||One storm spawned an EF1 tornado in the western parts of the state. The tornado initially touched down east of Yatesville Lake in Kentucky, then crossed into Wayne County in West Virginia. On the West Virginia side of the tornado path, an area of tree damage was noted and a house was slightly damaged at the intersection of Route 52 and Fort Gay Road. Some additional tree damage was observed further to the east up Fort Gay Road, which was estimated to be the end of the track.

Some additional tree damage was observed further to the east up Fort Gay Road, which was estimated to be the end of the track.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed tree was observed across Saye Lane along with power lines. Northwest of Coal City, a fallen tree limb downed another power line and poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms were observed in West Virginia on the afternoon of August 7th. Storms developed as a result of hot and humid conditions in advance of a cold front, then activity continued as the front crossed the region. Gusty winds within storms downed numerous trees and power lines, with a few instances of hail up to one and a half inch in size. Heavy rainfall led to flash flooding around Parkersburg and Lubeck in Wood County, and the towns of Lumberport and Bridgeport in Harrison County.||One storm spawned an EF1 tornado in the western parts of the state. The tornado initially touched down east of Yatesville Lake in Kentucky, then crossed into Wayne County in West Virginia. On the West Virginia side of the tornado path, an area of tree damage was noted and a house was slightly damaged at the intersection of Route 52 and Fort Gay Road. Some additional tree damage was observed further to the east up Fort Gay Road, which was estimated to be the end of the track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was knocked down in Spring Hill. Another tree was blown down a few miles down the road in a local South Charleston neighborhood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sagging cold front sluggishly pressed through West Virginia beginning on August 25th, continuing to shift eastward on the 26th. The slow nature of this boundary promoted an extended period of showers and thunderstorms spanning both days. ||Starting on the 25th, a few thunderstorms sprouted during the predawn hours through parts of north central West Virginia, with individual tree damage noted in each county. A few storms reached severe levels during the morning rush hour, with strong winds causing additional tree damage in Clay and Kanawha Counties. Within the storms passing over Kanawha County, bursts of heavier rainfall took place, and caused minor high water issues in known trouble areas in and around downtown Charleston. While the bulk of storms diminished shortly after sunrise, another round of flash flooding occurred in parts of Teays Valley in Putnam County and eastward near the Putnam/Kanawha County lines early that morning.||On the next day on August 26th, more rain fell across parts of eastern Kanawha County. The disturbance imposing the slow moving cold front finally set sail to the east on the 26th so convection chances quickly diminished. However, the ground conditions left behind by these two days of precipitation would set the stage for major flash flooding to take shape on August 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree fell down and laid across the road on Trap Springs Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing mid-level disturbance was the trigger for late-day and evening thunderstorms on August 12th. Strong to severe thunderstorms pressed in from southeast Ohio during the afternoon and passed through north central West Virginia. Main result from these storms were downed trees, but there was an instance of a developing funnel cloud around the Belleville area of Wood County. This was likely stemming from a tornado that had touched down in Athens County, Ohio before lifting back up into the storm. A cold front continued to encroach on the region in the evening, but after losing the diurnal heating component once the sun set, convection quickly waned over West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed along Route 18 near Alma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of August 28th, a ribbon of heavy showers spawned just above the southern West Virginia coalfields and propagated northeastward into the central portions of the state. An inverted surface trough feature festered over the Central Appalachians and was the primary culprit behind this swath of slow moving and heavy rainfall. Precipitation amounts recorded by local weather stations were on the upward of 3 to 5 inches from Lincoln and Boone County all the way up to Braxton County. This brought upon an extensive amount of flood damage across the state as creeks and streams were quickly inundated with water, spilling onto local roads and into nearby homes. Previous rainfall from August 26th did not help matters, as the ground had little time to recover and could not support the quick inundation of rain on the morning of the 28th.||The hardest hit area from this event took place in eastern Kanawha County, where the Winifrede, Coal Grove, and Chelyan areas observed million dollars worth of flood damage. An NWS Storm Survey team went out to these areas the next day to see first hand the amount of damage caused by the flooding. In Kanawha County alone, 3 homes were deemed destroyed, along with a fire station and a local church. An additional 50 to 100 homes observed at least minor to major flooding due to the close proximity of the houses to the swollen creeks, including Slaughter Creek, Kelleys Creek, Little Creek, and Fields Creek. Several cars were swept up by the floodwaters and became submerged in the creeks, while many of the roads within the local communities were washed out and destroyed. At the time of the Storm Survey, crews from the West Virginia Department of Highways were diligently working to put down temporary gravel in order to allow trapped residents to be able to safely evacuate. A section of railroad tracks were washed out and destroyed in Winifrede near the destroyed volunteer fire station along Fields Creek. Numerous sheds and trailer homes were lifted off of their foundations and moved several feet by the fast moving water. Many of the creeks remained out of their banks into the afternoon and evening hours on the 28th, with the restoration process ongoing for days to weeks to come due to the severity of the damage.||In Calhoun County, flash flooding took place as a result of the headwaters of the Little Kanawha River. This yielded a handful of homes observing major flooding damage. The Little Kanawha remained out of its banks into the evening of August 28th, continuing to spill water onto portions of Route 16. Over in Clay County, a few trouble spots arose as several creeks rose out of their banks and caused high water issues along nearby roadways.||From a statewide perspective, around 40 private bridges were destroyed in West Virginia. Over 5 million dollars worth of damage was noted across the hardest hit areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Danville, Drawdy Creek rose out of its banks and inflicted water to spill onto the roadway at Arvle Lane. A water rescue was conducted to help a citizen trapped in their home. Two other water rescues occurred along Coal River Road in the town of Seth. Sections of Three Fork Road were washed out along with several culverts in the Hopkins Fork area. At least 7 homes were impacted by high water issues in the county, with two of those homes deemed to have observed major flooding impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After major flash flooding took place across parts of the state the day before, additional flash flooding occurred on the afternoon of August 29th in Harrison County. Showers and a few embedded thunderstorms passed through parts of north central West Virginia as sufficient tropical moisture festered over the area. This led to locally very heavy rainfall in parts of Harrison and Taylor Counties. ||Harrison County felt the brunt of the urban flash flooding on this day, with over a dozen road closures and at least 11 homes suffering major damage. A townhome apartment complex in Bridgeport sustained minor to major flooding in the first floor of the homes, with restoration assistance needed from professional cleaners and other vendors. Even beyond the 29th, crew from the West Virginia Department of Highways were busy handling washed out roads and culvert replacements as a result of the inundating water from the rising creeks and streams.||Precipitation largely tapered down after midnight as drier air filtered in and precipitable water values substantially dropped off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing flooding continued along Hawk Highway in Lost Creek into the evening as high water slowly receded. The Bridgeport area also continued to see high water concerns after showers and storms finally departed out of Harrison County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front, driven by an unusually strong upper-level trough and wind shear, interacted with a hot, humid, and unstable airmass over the Mid-Atlantic. This pattern resulted in the formation of lines of thunderstorms and several supercells. The most widespread and significant wind damage was noted over northern Maryland, although pockets of considerable wind damage were noted over portions of northwestern Virginia and eastern West Virginia. Somewhat atypical for the Mid-Atlantic is late summer was the number of large hail reports, with multiple instances of giant hail noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported near the intersection of Cedar Ridge Road and Teaberry Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms were observed in West Virginia on the afternoon of August 7th. Storms developed as a result of hot and humid conditions in advance of a cold front, then activity continued as the front crossed the region. Gusty winds within storms downed numerous trees and power lines, with a few instances of hail up to one and a half inch in size. Heavy rainfall led to flash flooding around Parkersburg and Lubeck in Wood County, and the towns of Lumberport and Bridgeport in Harrison County.||One storm spawned an EF1 tornado in the western parts of the state. The tornado initially touched down east of Yatesville Lake in Kentucky, then crossed into Wayne County in West Virginia. On the West Virginia side of the tornado path, an area of tree damage was noted and a house was slightly damaged at the intersection of Route 52 and Fort Gay Road. Some additional tree damage was observed further to the east up Fort Gay Road, which was estimated to be the end of the track.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing mid-level disturbance was the trigger for late-day and evening thunderstorms on August 12th. Strong to severe thunderstorms pressed in from southeast Ohio during the afternoon and passed through north central West Virginia. Main result from these storms were downed trees, but there was an instance of a developing funnel cloud around the Belleville area of Wood County. This was likely stemming from a tornado that had touched down in Athens County, Ohio before lifting back up into the storm. A cold front continued to encroach on the region in the evening, but after losing the diurnal heating component once the sun set, convection quickly waned over West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed onto Berry Run Road north of Flemington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sagging cold front sluggishly pressed through West Virginia beginning on August 25th, continuing to shift eastward on the 26th. The slow nature of this boundary promoted an extended period of showers and thunderstorms spanning both days. ||Starting on the 25th, a few thunderstorms sprouted during the predawn hours through parts of north central West Virginia, with individual tree damage noted in each county. A few storms reached severe levels during the morning rush hour, with strong winds causing additional tree damage in Clay and Kanawha Counties. Within the storms passing over Kanawha County, bursts of heavier rainfall took place, and caused minor high water issues in known trouble areas in and around downtown Charleston. While the bulk of storms diminished shortly after sunrise, another round of flash flooding occurred in parts of Teays Valley in Putnam County and eastward near the Putnam/Kanawha County lines early that morning.||On the next day on August 26th, more rain fell across parts of eastern Kanawha County. The disturbance imposing the slow moving cold front finally set sail to the east on the 26th so convection chances quickly diminished. However, the ground conditions left behind by these two days of precipitation would set the stage for major flash flooding to take shape on August 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at the intersection of Maccorkle Avenue and Paint Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of August 28th, a ribbon of heavy showers spawned just above the southern West Virginia coalfields and propagated northeastward into the central portions of the state. An inverted surface trough feature festered over the Central Appalachians and was the primary culprit behind this swath of slow moving and heavy rainfall. Precipitation amounts recorded by local weather stations were on the upward of 3 to 5 inches from Lincoln and Boone County all the way up to Braxton County. This brought upon an extensive amount of flood damage across the state as creeks and streams were quickly inundated with water, spilling onto local roads and into nearby homes. Previous rainfall from August 26th did not help matters, as the ground had little time to recover and could not support the quick inundation of rain on the morning of the 28th.||The hardest hit area from this event took place in eastern Kanawha County, where the Winifrede, Coal Grove, and Chelyan areas observed million dollars worth of flood damage. An NWS Storm Survey team went out to these areas the next day to see first hand the amount of damage caused by the flooding. In Kanawha County alone, 3 homes were deemed destroyed, along with a fire station and a local church. An additional 50 to 100 homes observed at least minor to major flooding due to the close proximity of the houses to the swollen creeks, including Slaughter Creek, Kelleys Creek, Little Creek, and Fields Creek. Several cars were swept up by the floodwaters and became submerged in the creeks, while many of the roads within the local communities were washed out and destroyed. At the time of the Storm Survey, crews from the West Virginia Department of Highways were diligently working to put down temporary gravel in order to allow trapped residents to be able to safely evacuate. A section of railroad tracks were washed out and destroyed in Winifrede near the destroyed volunteer fire station along Fields Creek. Numerous sheds and trailer homes were lifted off of their foundations and moved several feet by the fast moving water. Many of the creeks remained out of their banks into the afternoon and evening hours on the 28th, with the restoration process ongoing for days to weeks to come due to the severity of the damage.||In Calhoun County, flash flooding took place as a result of the headwaters of the Little Kanawha River. This yielded a handful of homes observing major flooding damage. The Little Kanawha remained out of its banks into the evening of August 28th, continuing to spill water onto portions of Route 16. Over in Clay County, a few trouble spots arose as several creeks rose out of their banks and caused high water issues along nearby roadways.||From a statewide perspective, around 40 private bridges were destroyed in West Virginia. Over 5 million dollars worth of damage was noted across the hardest hit areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stinson Creek and the West Fork of the Little Kanawha River rose out of its banks and caused flooding to occur at the intersection of Route 16 and Stinson Road near the Stinson grocery store. Several people were trapped in their homes due to high water between Minnora and Chloe along Route 16. Around eight homes were affected by minor flooding issues. Also along Route 16, up to two feet of water was observed in the Chloe Auto and Hardware store.

An additional 50 to 100 homes observed at least minor to major flooding due to the close proximity of the houses to the swollen creeks, including Slaughter Creek, Kelleys Creek, Little Creek, and Fields Creek. Several cars were swept up by the floodwaters and became submerged in the creeks, while many of the roads within the local communities were washed out and destroyed. At the time of the Storm Survey, crews from the West Virginia Department of Highways were diligently working to put down temporary gravel in order to allow trapped residents to be able to safely evacuate. A section of railroad tracks were washed out and destroyed in Winifrede near the destroyed volunteer fire station along Fields Creek. Numerous sheds and trailer homes were lifted off of their foundations and moved several feet by the fast moving water. Many of the creeks remained out of their banks into the afternoon and evening hours on the 28th, with the restoration process ongoing for days to weeks to come due to the severity of the damage.||In Calhoun County, flash flooding took place as a result of the headwaters of the Little Kanawha River. This yielded a handful of homes observing major flooding damage. The Little Kanawha remained out of its banks into the evening of August 28th, continuing to spill water onto portions of Route 16. Over in Clay County, a few trouble spots arose as several creeks rose out of their banks and caused high water issues along nearby roadways.||From a statewide perspective, around 40 private bridges were destroyed in West Virginia. Over 5 million dollars worth of damage was noted across the hardest hit areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flooding damage was observed in different neighborhoods within the Chelyan and Cedar Grove townships. ||On the Cedar Grove side of the Kanawha River, many homes were surrounded by water as Kelleys Creek rose out of its banks. The NWS Storm Survey crew noted a few areas along Horsemill Hollow Road that were washed out as the creek curved in and around the roadway. Department of Highways crews were quickly attempting to put gravel on the road to ensure a way for citizens that live up along Horsemill Hollow Road to evacuate, otherwise they were trapped due to the obstruction of the road. A water rescue was conducted on William Street as parts of East Dupont Avenue also were inundated with water.||In Witcher Creek, main structural damage was noted to be storage sheds coming off of its foundation due to the intensity of the high water, some of which also entered into a trailer home along Witcher Creek Road. This house was deemed to have major damage as much of the items of the home were destroyed and being thrown out. A handful of cars were also lodged into Witcher Creek and were waiting to be removed as the Storm Survey team passed through the area.||Traveling up the road along Route 60, a few other trouble spots arose in between Chesapeake, Belle, and Dickinson. High water laid along the parking lot of Riverside High School, causing the school, and other local elementary schools that dealt with high water issues, to close for the day.||Another area that was hit hard by the heavy rain was on the other side of the Kanawha River in Chelyan. Slaughter Creek and Little Creek quickly rose out of their banks and resulted in major flooding along the local roadways, including Slaughter Creek Road, Little Creek Road, Cooper Hollow Road, and Fields Creek Road. This area of the county was where the most substantial flooding seemed to have occurred, with three local homes destroyed due to the high water levels. ||In the area of Fields Creek, a trailer was ripped off of its foundation at the entrance of Fields Creek Road and looked to have been transported a few feet away when the creek was out of its banks. Several cars were submerged in the mud along Fields Creek Road and a few low lying homes observed major flooding in their basements and garages. Old Glory Road was also completely washed out.||Due to the close proximity of Little Creek Church to Little Creek, the damage was noted to be unsalvageable and would likely have to be demolished. The Chesapeake Volunteer Fire Department, located along Cooper Hollow Road and Old Glory Road, suffered substantial structural damage as a result of the creek rising out of its banks, most likely condemning the building along with losing the fire engine that was stored in the garage of the station. Local residents that live up Cooper Hollow Road were trapped for over 24 hours in their homes as a result of the road becoming washed out.  ||Outside of the three homes, fire station, and church being destroyed, 22 homes were deemed to have major flooding damage, and an additional 88 homes who observed minor flooding issues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After major flash flooding took place across parts of the state the day before, additional flash flooding occurred on the afternoon of August 29th in Harrison County. Showers and a few embedded thunderstorms passed through parts of north central West Virginia as sufficient tropical moisture festered over the area. This led to locally very heavy rainfall in parts of Harrison and Taylor Counties. ||Harrison County felt the brunt of the urban flash flooding on this day, with over a dozen road closures and at least 11 homes suffering major damage. A townhome apartment complex in Bridgeport sustained minor to major flooding in the first floor of the homes, with restoration assistance needed from professional cleaners and other vendors. Even beyond the 29th, crew from the West Virginia Department of Highways were busy handling washed out roads and culvert replacements as a result of the inundating water from the rising creeks and streams.||Precipitation largely tapered down after midnight as drier air filtered in and precipitable water values substantially dropped off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple areas within Harrison County observed flooded roadways, including Bridgeport, Clarksburg, Stonewood, and Anmoore. Water rescues were conducted along Anmoore Road in Anmoore and along Hawk Highway in Lost Creek due to the bad road conditions. Due to the high water, over a dozen roads were closed for a period of time. ||At least 11 homes suffered major flooding damage in the county, including the Willow Greene neighborhood. This Bridgeport apartment complex observed water damage to the first floor of the townhomes, with many residents having to throw out furniture and prized possessions. Professional cleaners and restoration vendors were called to the complex to help with the clean up process. Over 100 more homes were affected by flooding as well. Crews from the West Virginia Department of Highways continued to handle shoulder washouts, pipe cleanups, and stone placements in culverts days after the heavy rainfall event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing mid-level disturbance was the trigger for late-day and evening thunderstorms on August 12th. Strong to severe thunderstorms pressed in from southeast Ohio during the afternoon and passed through north central West Virginia. Main result from these storms were downed trees, but there was an instance of a developing funnel cloud around the Belleville area of Wood County. This was likely stemming from a tornado that had touched down in Athens County, Ohio before lifting back up into the storm. A cold front continued to encroach on the region in the evening, but after losing the diurnal heating component once the sun set, convection quickly waned over West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down along State Route 23 near Shirley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front, driven by an unusually strong upper-level trough and wind shear, interacted with a hot, humid, and unstable airmass over the Mid-Atlantic. This pattern resulted in the formation of lines of thunderstorms and several supercells. The most widespread and significant wind damage was noted over northern Maryland, although pockets of considerable wind damage were noted over portions of northwestern Virginia and eastern West Virginia. Somewhat atypical for the Mid-Atlantic is late summer was the number of large hail reports, with multiple instances of giant hail noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees blew down in Elk Garden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms were observed in West Virginia on the afternoon of August 7th. Storms developed as a result of hot and humid conditions in advance of a cold front, then activity continued as the front crossed the region. Gusty winds within storms downed numerous trees and power lines, with a few instances of hail up to one and a half inch in size. Heavy rainfall led to flash flooding around Parkersburg and Lubeck in Wood County, and the towns of Lumberport and Bridgeport in Harrison County.||One storm spawned an EF1 tornado in the western parts of the state. The tornado initially touched down east of Yatesville Lake in Kentucky, then crossed into Wayne County in West Virginia. On the West Virginia side of the tornado path, an area of tree damage was noted and a house was slightly damaged at the intersection of Route 52 and Fort Gay Road. Some additional tree damage was observed further to the east up Fort Gay Road, which was estimated to be the end of the track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in the area of Valley Bend.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sagging cold front sluggishly pressed through West Virginia beginning on August 25th, continuing to shift eastward on the 26th. The slow nature of this boundary promoted an extended period of showers and thunderstorms spanning both days. ||Starting on the 25th, a few thunderstorms sprouted during the predawn hours through parts of north central West Virginia, with individual tree damage noted in each county. A few storms reached severe levels during the morning rush hour, with strong winds causing additional tree damage in Clay and Kanawha Counties. Within the storms passing over Kanawha County, bursts of heavier rainfall took place, and caused minor high water issues in known trouble areas in and around downtown Charleston. While the bulk of storms diminished shortly after sunrise, another round of flash flooding occurred in parts of Teays Valley in Putnam County and eastward near the Putnam/Kanawha County lines early that morning.||On the next day on August 26th, more rain fell across parts of eastern Kanawha County. The disturbance imposing the slow moving cold front finally set sail to the east on the 26th so convection chances quickly diminished. However, the ground conditions left behind by these two days of precipitation would set the stage for major flash flooding to take shape on August 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown in South Charleston at the intersection of Maccorkle Avenue and Chesapeake Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front dropping south from the Ohio Valley. As this front encountered an airmass with modest shear and very unstable conditions, it allowed allowed for afternoon thunderstorms to develop. Some of these storms produced damaging winds in western Greenbrier county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed one tree in Rupert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms passed across Mercer County during the early morning hours of July 19th, producing anywhere from 1.75 inches to 2.25 inches of rainfall within a hour.  Automated rain gages indicated that rainfall rates at times were in excess of 3 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flooded the Midway Tunnel on Bramwell Road at least 3 feet deep.  One automobile became stranded in the flood waters.  The driver was able to evacuate the car.  Property damage is the estimated damage to the car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms passed across Mercer County during the early morning hours of July 19th, producing anywhere from 1.75 inches to 2.25 inches of rainfall within a hour.  Automated rain gages indicated that rainfall rates at times were in excess of 3 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grassy Branch was observed flowing out of its banks and across Grassy Branch Road at least 6 inches deep.  No damage was reported to the road, and it was open to traffic after the flooding receded.
